content:
In https://blog.buenzli.dev/rust-for-linux-first-contrib/ Remo shows his driver for FPD-Link III deserializer. His code uses i2c and regmap abstractions, but I do see them on rust-next. Where I can find branches or patches with them?

Aside above: Am I using right channel for such questions?

Mainline does not have a lot of things that people have worked or are working on. I would suggest searching for the terms in https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/ first, since nowadays most of the work happens there, as it should.

Of course, Cc @Remo Senekowitsch who can probably tell you more about what code he actually used, if it was not from the list.
I hope that helps!
Thanks, @Miguel Ojeda. Reading #General > DS90UB954 driver done, ready to upstream? for starter...

Hi Michal. (I did get your email, just didn't get around to respond yet.) You are correct that the rust-next branch comes from the Rust-for-Linux fork and I merged Raspberry Pi support from their fork. I2C and regmap abstractions are from Fabien Parent's branch: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/tree/b4/ncv6336. The GPIO abstractions are cherry-picked from this commit: https://github.com/Fabo/linux/commit/6ece6125a6839040af4d146ab1f96c4f1a3cceb0. Hope that helps!

Thanks, @Remo Senekowitsch - these are helpful references. I need to cherry-pick these to my branch for starter.
Do you or @Miguel Ojeda know is anyone working on VL42 abstractions? DS90UB954 driver doesn't seem to implement any V4L2 operations, like start/stop streaming, frame format and pixel format selection.
@Michał Fita I would check the mailing list too. @Daniel Almeida may know the latest status on V4L2.
@Michał Fita Mauro would like to see the data that bringing rust to v4l2 helps in any way. There is no one working on this at the moment.

I merged i2c and regmap from b4/ncv6336 for starter. But now another newbie question as I'm confused and probably did something wrong:
In rust/kernel/i2c.rs line 235 I'm getting error there's not .cast_mut() method, while my rust-analyzer shows there's one in core::ptr::const_ptr. Keep in mind I'm cross compiling with LLVM=1 ARCH=arm64 CROSS_COMPILE=aarch64-linux-gnu-.
OK, removal of .cast_mut() worked :thinking:

The I2C and regmap abstractions were sent to the ML a while ago, but I haven't updated the patches for a while. I was busy before because of the addition of a new member to my family, but things are settling and I think I should be able to revise some of the patches and send new version hopfuly later this month.

I needed some clean ups, but nothing major. Changes from @Remo Senekowitsch are worth considering to include, but I added const to pub fn with_access_ops<T: ConfigOps>(mut self) -> Self so I can have builder chain in the const context. config_with!() macro has const.
The only thing I need to crack for sure, @Fabien Parent, is that if this would work with registers of various sizes - driver I'm concerned about need to access values of lengths from byte to 4 bytes. In the original C version I wrote my own mechanism as in 5.16 regmap was quite limited. That kernel version is result of highest supported kernels for various SBC, Texas Instruments is significantly staying behind.

If I'm after fwnode_graph_get_next_endpoint() should I look at adding it to FwNode in driver/property.rs or rather a trait to extend FwNode if needed? I added bindings from linux/of_graph.h but not sure what's the abstractive relationship of that header file to linux/property.h.
Suggestions?

fwnode_graph_get_next_endpoint is from property.c, right? So, property.rs seems like a reasonable place to put the binding. But I recommend just getting things working first, moving bindings around later to clean things up is not a big deal.
@Remo Senekowitsch are there any thests for anything in property.rs?
Yes, in: samples/rust/rust_driver_platform.rs.

test-device@2 is the only device-tree used for this?
If I want to test my endpoints() iterator, I need to extend that?
And am I _reading_ it right, I need to build and load that driver? There's nothing atm as unit tests?
I don't know what your endpoints() iterator is. Apart from that, it sounds right.
In principle, as childrens() in calls fwnode_graph_get_next_endpoint()...
